channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-19566] FLR-EC: mark EC OST objects for LFSCK, rebuild EC components
item.description:
During LFSCK layout reconstruction, it may rebuild a corrupted LOV EA on the MDT based only on the trusted.fid information stored on each OST object. The client will need to pass a flag to the OST objects (e.g. during EC writes) to mark them as EC parity and not regular data.

LFSCK in turn needs to check trusted.fid for this flag on the OST objects, and so that the rebuilt MDS layout rebuild process marks the reconstructed component/EC mirror with LOV_PATTERN_PARITY and not use it as a regular data mirror after LFSCK rebuilds the layout.
item.summary: FLR-EC: mark EC OST objects for LFSCK, rebuild EC components

status.description: This issue is being actively worked on at the moment by the assignee.

comments.comment:
"Patrick Farrell <pfarrell@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/62489
Subject: LU-19566 tests: test EC with lfsck
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 5e9d279d08cdf32ff8703f36f00b54ccb6c16d1c

I think it is fine if the unwritten OST objects are not restored, at least they will not provide random garbage on read. It is OK to set the PARITY flag on these objects when they are first written.

While it is true that FLR mirrors have the same behavior, the vast majority of usage creates the mirrors only with "lfs mirror extend" so they are not attached to the file until after the data has been sync'd. I think it is pretty rare to use a default layout that has multiple mirrors defined, and even more rare for them to become corrupted and need LFSCK to rebuild the layout.

For LFSCK layout rebuild, this issue could be fixed by marking the components for unused objects (with zero size and 07666 permission) as STALE. It might be possible to use the layout version on the objects, or the smaller mirror (presumably with less data written to it), to determine if a mirror with written data is stale. Or if they are all the same size then arbitrarily pick one and mark the others STALE, so they will be resync'd afterward.

Ah, this is interesting.

Filter fid on the OST is populated on first write, but we don't write to parity components immediately.  So lfsck doesn't restore them in this case.

But after investigating it, this is exactly what happens with mirrored files.  If you have two mirrors and you never write to the second one (ie, never call resync), it is NOT restored.  The file loses the mirror.

So this is the same as how parity mirrors work.  Is that acceptable - lfsck won't restore the parity components unless parity has been written to.  There's not a good way around it and it's the same behavior as FLR mirrors, so I think it's probably fine...?

"Patrick Farrell <pfarrell@whamcloud.com>" uploaded a new patch: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/62389
Subject: LU-19566 lustre: add ol_comp_flags to ost_layout
Project: fs/lustre-release
Branch: master
Current Patch Set: 1
Commit: 21767b64885372a0935ddeecc7226b6a79d54421

Reflecting on this, obviously we could create a single LMAC flag for parity...  But don't we want to store the other flags, like stale, as well?  I know we don't today but storing and restoring STALE seems important; obviously PREFER is just a nice-to-have.

But anyway, in this case we should extend the ost_layout (seems like the place to go to me) with a flags field and transfer all of the flags, I think.

I'm leaning that way.

There are a number of options here. The struct filter_fid is indeed the place where most of this metadata lives, and has seen various updates over the years as new parts of the file layout have been added. There is struct lustre_ost_attrs that holds the other part of the layout information on an object, but it is somewhat redundant with struct ost_layout and TBH I don't recall which one is the "current favorite" for adding new fields.

If we only need a single flag, then we could add LMAC_PARITY or similar? We don't really want to make the parity component impossible to access if the OST is downgraded (which an "LMAI_PARITY" would achieve, incompat vs. compat).
I don't have a lot of practice worming my way into existing on disk formats, but I don't think there's a flags field today, so I think we're looking at changing filter fid.  Thoughts?
Do we do this for any other object type, eg, compression?  Asking to see if there's code to reference.
